subsections.title: JVM Architecture & Class Loading
subsections.keyRule: JVM = Class Loader + Runtime Data Areas (Heap, Stack, Metaspace, PC Registers) + Execution Engine (Interpreter + JIT + GC). Class loading: Bootstrap → Extension → Application ClassLoader. Classes loaded once per ClassLoader — duplicate loaders = duplicate classes.

terms.what: Class Loader finds and loads .class files into JVM memory. Three built-in loaders: Bootstrap (rt.jar/JDK), Extension (ext/ directory), Application (your classpath).
terms.why: JVM does not load all classes at startup — classes are loaded on first use (lazy loading). Class Loaders make this possible.
terms.how: Parent delegation: when Application ClassLoader asked for a class, it asks Parent (Extension), which asks Parent (Bootstrap) first. If Bootstrap cannot find it, it comes back down. Prevents you from overriding java.lang.String.
terms.where: Every class load in JVM uses this chain. Custom ClassLoaders are used by: application servers (Tomcat), OSGi, hot-reload tools (JRebel).
terms.when: Custom ClassLoader needed when: loading classes from non-standard location, hot reloading, or class isolation in containers.
terms.issue: Same class loaded by two different ClassLoaders = two distinct classes in JVM — instanceof returns false even if same name. Causes ClassCastException.
terms.fix: Ensure only one ClassLoader loads a class. Common in application servers — check ClassLoader hierarchy in web.xml or container config.

star.s: ClassCastException on 'instanceof' check that seems correct.
star.a: Same class loaded by parent ClassLoader and webapp ClassLoader — two classes. Fixed ClassLoader config in Tomcat.

terms.what: JVM has several memory areas: Heap (objects), Stack (method frames per thread), Metaspace (class metadata), PC Register (current instruction per thread), Native Method Stack (native code).
terms.why: Understanding the areas helps you diagnose the right type of OOM, tune the right JVM flags, and reason about thread safety.
terms.how: Heap: -Xmx, -Xms. Metaspace: -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize. Stack: -Xss. PC Register and Native Stack are small and rarely tuned.
terms.where: Every running Java application uses all these areas. JVM flags control their sizes.
terms.when: Tune these when: diagnosing OOM errors (know which area), optimizing startup memory, sizing containers/pods.
terms.issue: 'OOM: Java heap space' vs 'OOM: Metaspace' vs 'OOM: unable to create native thread' — different areas, different causes, different fixes.
terms.fix: 'Heap space': increase -Xmx or fix memory leak. 'Metaspace': increase - XX:MaxMetaspaceSize or fix class loader leak. 'Native thread': reduce thread count or increase OS process limit.

star.s: App throws OOM but heap looks fine in monitoring.
star.a: Checked Metaspace — 100% full. Many plugins loading duplicate classes. Set - XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=512m and fixed class loader leak.

subsections.title: Garbage Collection Algorithms
subsections.keyRule: GC Types: Serial (single thread, for small apps), Parallel (multi-thread, high throughput), G1GC (balanced, default Java 9+), ZGC/Shenandoah (low pause, Java 11+). Key metrics: throughput (% time not in GC) and pause time (stop-the-world duration). Choose based on your app's priorities.

terms.what: ZGC (Z Garbage Collector) is a low-latency GC with pause times under 1 millisecond regardless of heap size — even terabyte heaps.
terms.why: Some apps need consistent sub-millisecond latency — trading systems, real-time games, latency-sensitive microservices. G1GC cannot guarantee this.
terms.how: ZGC does almost all work concurrently with application threads. Uses colored pointers and load barriers. Pause time is nearly constant regardless of heap size.
terms.where: High-frequency trading, real-time systems, large-heap apps needing predictable latency.
terms.when: Use ZGC for: latency SLA under 10ms, heaps over 32GB, any latency-critical app. Note: slightly lower throughput than Parallel GC.
terms.issue: ZGC requires more CPU and memory overhead than G1GC — running concurrent GC threads has cost.
terms.fix: ZGC on Java 15+ is production-ready. Enable with -XX:+UseZGC. Monitor CPU overhead and ensure your environment has enough CPUs for concurrent GC.

star.s: Financial app has GC pause spikes of 500ms — unacceptable for trading.

terms.what: Heap is split into Young Generation (new objects) and Old Generation (long-lived objects). Most objects die young — collected quickly in Young Gen (Minor GC). Survivors promoted to Old Gen.
terms.why: Most objects are very short-lived (method-local, temporary). Collecting them separately is fast. Mixing old and new in same space wastes time collecting long-lived objects repeatedly.
terms.how: Young Gen: Eden + two Survivor spaces (S0, S1). New objects go to Eden. Minor GC copies survivors to S0/S1. After N survivals (default 15), promoted to Old Gen. Old Gen collected by Major/Full GC — slow.
terms.where: G1GC still has Young/Old concept but in regions. Understanding this helps tune - XX:NewRatio and -XX:SurvivorRatio.
terms.when: Tune generation sizes when: Minor GC happens too frequently (Young Gen too small), or objects promote too fast (Survivor space too small).
terms.issue: Objects promote to Old Gen too quickly — too many survivors fill Survivor space. Old Gen fills up faster, requiring expensive Full GC.
terms.fix: Increase Survivor space: -XX:SurvivorRatio=8. Increase Young Gen: - XX:NewRatio=2 (young:old = 1:2). Or increase -Xmx to give more total heap.

star.s: Frequent Full GC causing 2-second pauses every minute.
star.a: GC log shows objects promoted to Old Gen immediately — Survivor space full. Increased Young Gen.
star.r: Full GC every 30 minutes instead of 1 minute. Pauses rare.

terms.what: GC flags control heap sizes, GC algorithm, and behavior. Right flags make the difference between a stable app and OOM or pause-heavy app.
terms.why: Default JVM settings are conservative. Production apps need explicit tuning for their specific workload.
terms.how: Key flags: -Xmx4g (max heap 4GB), -Xms1g (start 1GB), -XX:+UseG1GC (G1), - XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200 (target pause), -Xlog:gc* (GC logging), - XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError (dump on crash).
terms.where: JVM startup flags in Dockerfile, shell scripts, application server config, Kubernetes resource limits.
terms.when: Set flags before any production deployment. Review GC logs regularly. Never go to production without explicit -Xmx.
terms.issue: Not setting -Xmx = JVM uses up to 25% of physical RAM — unpredictable in containers. Container kills process when memory limit exceeded without warning.
terms.fix: In Docker: -Xmx should be 75% of container memory limit. Java 10+ auto-detects container limits. Use -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 for dynamic sizing.

star.s: Kubernetes pod killed by OOM killer despite Java OOM never thrown.
star.a: JVM heap was 25% of physical RAM but container limit was 512MB — JVM used too much. Added -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.
star.r: JVM respects container limit. No more pod kills.

terms.what: Memory leak in Java means objects are still referenced (reachable) but are no longer needed — GC cannot collect them. Memory grows until OOM.
terms.why: Java GC is not magic — it only collects unreachable objects. If old code holds a reference to an object 'just in case', that object lives forever.
terms.how: Common causes: 1) Static collections that grow (static Map never cleared). 2) Non- closed resources (streams, connections). 3) Listeners not unregistered. 4) ThreadLocal not removed.
terms.where: Long-running services: web apps, microservices, batch processors.
terms.when: Diagnose when: memory grows steadily over time, heap dump shows same object type growing, app runs fine for hours then crashes.
terms.issue: Memory leaks are slow — might take hours or days to manifest. Hard to catch in short tests.
terms.fix: 1) -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError — capture dump on crash. 2) Eclipse MAT to analyze — find dominator tree. 3) WeakHashMap or Caffeine cache for automatic eviction. 4) Always unregister listeners. 5) Remove ThreadLocal after request.

star.s: Memory grows 10MB per hour — stable for days then OOM.
star.a: Eclipse MAT heap dump showed EventListeners accumulating — listeners added on each API call, never removed. Fixed lifecycle.

terms.what: Method Area (called Metaspace in Java 8+) stores class-level data: class structure, method bytecode, static variables, constant pool. Shared across all threads.
terms.why: Class metadata must be stored somewhere outside the Heap — one copy per class, not per instance.
terms.how: Metaspace grows dynamically in native memory (no fixed PermGen limit like before Java 8). Tune with -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=512m.
terms.where: Every class loaded adds to Metaspace. Dynamic proxy generation (Spring AOP, Hibernate, CGLIB) creates many classes.
terms.when: Check Metaspace when: 'OOM: Metaspace' errors, monitoring shows Metaspace growing, or many frameworks load dynamic classes.
terms.issue: Frameworks generating proxy classes at runtime (Spring, Hibernate, Mockito) can fill Metaspace. ClassLoader leaks in hot-reload scenarios.

terms.what: Each thread has its own PC (Program Counter) Register — holds the address of the JVM instruction currently executing. For native methods, PC is undefined.
terms.why: Enables thread context switching — when scheduler pauses a thread and resumes it, PC tells JVM where to continue execution.
terms.how: PC is managed entirely by JVM — developers have no direct control or access. Each thread's PC advances through bytecode.
terms.where: Implicit in every thread. GC does not touch PC. Thread dumps show the current execution point which corresponds to PC.
terms.when: Purely internal JVM mechanism. You never tune it. Relevant only for understanding JVM internals in interviews.
terms.issue: Not a cause of any common production issue — purely internal.

star.s: Interview: what does each thread have its own copy of in JVM?
star.a: Stack, PC Register, and Native Method Stack. Heap and Method Area are shared.

terms.what: Native Method Stack is the call stack for native (non-Java) methods invoked via JNI (Java Native Interface). Separate from the Java Stack.
terms.why: When Java calls C/C++ code through JNI, the native code needs its own stack. JVM maintains it separately.
terms.how: Automatic — used when your code or a library calls native methods. java.io, java.net, many JDK internals use JNI underneath.
terms.where: JNI calls, native libraries (OpenSSL, database drivers using native protocols), JVM internal operations.
terms.when: Relevant when debugging native crashes (JVM crash log shows native stack traces), or investigating memory usage from native libraries.
terms.issue: Native code crash (SIGSEGV, SIGBUS) shows as JVM crash with hs_err_pid file — different debugging approach than Java exceptions.
terms.fix: Check hs_err_pid log for native crash. Update native library version. Check JNI code for memory management errors.

star.s: JVM crashes with no Java exception — hs_err_pid file.
star.a: Native library (OpenSSL old version) has buffer overflow. Updated to current version.

subsections.title: GC Deep Dive
subsections.keyRule: GC phases: Mark (find reachable objects via GC roots) → Sweep (reclaim unreachable) → Compact (defragment). Stop-The-World pauses happen when GC must freeze threads. TLAB (Thread-Local Allocation Buffer) speeds allocation by giving each thread its own Eden slice.

terms.what: GC Roots are the starting points for GC reachability analysis. An object is live (not garbage) if it is reachable from any GC Root via a chain of references.
terms.why: GC needs a starting point. It starts from roots and marks everything reachable as live. Anything not reachable = garbage.
terms.how: GC Roots include: 1) Local variables in method frames. 2) Static fields. 3) JNI references. 4) Active threads themselves. 5) Synchronized monitors.

terms.what: Three GC phases: Mark = traverse from GC roots, mark all reachable objects as live. Sweep = reclaim memory of all unmarked objects. Compact = move live objects together, eliminate fragmentation.
terms.why: Sweep alone leaves 'holes' in memory — fragmentation. Compact solves this but requires moving objects and updating all references.
terms.how: Mark: traverse object graph, set flag. Sweep: walk heap, free unmarked. Compact: slide live objects to one end of heap. Serial GC does all three. G1GC compacts incrementally.
terms.where: All garbage collectors use some form of mark-sweep. Compact is done by G1GC, ZGC, Shenandoah in concurrent phases.
terms.when: Compaction matters when: heap is fragmented (many small holes), large object allocations fail despite 'enough' free memory.
terms.issue: Compaction requires updating all references to moved objects — expensive. This is a major source of pause time.
terms.fix: G1GC and ZGC compact concurrently or incrementally to minimize pause. ZGC uses colored pointers to track moved objects without stop-the-world.

star.s: Large object allocations fail despite showing 2GB free heap.
star.a: Heap fragmentation — free space in many small pieces. Switched to G1GC with compaction.

terms.what: Stop-The-World pause: GC freezes ALL application threads for a GC phase. No code runs during STW. Users see latency spike.
terms.why: Some GC phases require consistent view of the heap — cannot have application threads moving objects while GC marks them.
terms.how: JVM sends a safepoint signal. All threads reach a safepoint and stop. GC runs. JVM releases. Threads resume.
terms.where: All GC algorithms have some STW. G1GC STW is milliseconds. ZGC STW is sub- millisecond. Serial/Parallel GC STW is seconds.
terms.when: Monitor STW pause time in GC logs: [GC Pause Young/Mixed/Full ...ms]. Optimize when pauses cause latency SLA violations.
terms.issue: Full GC on a 32GB heap can pause for 10-30 seconds — catastrophic for latency-sensitive services.
terms.fix: Move to G1GC or ZGC. Reduce heap fragmentation. Reduce object allocation rate. Fix memory leaks that force Full GC.

star.s: P99 latency spikes to 5 seconds every hour.
star.a: GC logs show Full GC of 32GB heap taking 4 seconds at same interval. Switched to ZGC.

terms.what: TLAB gives each thread a private slice of Eden space for object allocation. Threads allocate into their TLAB without any synchronization — fast allocation.
terms.why: Without TLAB, every 'new' object requires synchronized access to the shared Eden space — massive contention. TLAB eliminates this.
terms.how: Each thread gets a TLAB from Eden. Allocates within it via simple pointer bump — O(1), no lock. When TLAB is full, requests a new one from Eden (rare synchronized step).

terms.what: Serial GC: single thread, Stop-The-World — for small/desktop apps. Parallel GC: multiple threads, Stop-The-World — max throughput for batch. CMS: concurrent mark- sweep — low pause, deprecated in Java 9+.
terms.why: Different workloads need different GC trade-offs: throughput vs latency vs memory.
terms.how: -XX:+UseSerialGC — serial (one thread). -XX:+UseParallelGC — parallel (default before Java 9). -XX:+UseConcMarkSweepGC — CMS (deprecated). For new apps: G1GC (-XX:+UseG1GC) or ZGC.
terms.where: Serial: local dev/testing. Parallel: batch jobs that don't care about pause. CMS: old low-latency apps. G1GC: most production. ZGC: ultra-low latency.
terms.when: Choose GC based on: latency requirements, heap size, and whether throughput or pause time is more important.
terms.issue: CMS deprecated and removed in Java 14. Using -XX:+UseConcMarkSweepGC on Java 14+ = error.
terms.fix: Migrate CMS applications to G1GC or ZGC. G1GC covers most CMS use cases with better stability.

star.s: Batch job GC taking 30% of CPU.
star.a: Switched to Parallel GC. More GC threads.
star.r: GC time reduced to 8% of total. Batch job 25% faster.

terms.what: Shenandoah: concurrent GC by Red Hat, extremely low pause (sub-millisecond), available since Java 12 (OpenJDK). Epsilon: no-op GC — does NOT collect garbage. For testing and allocation-free apps.
terms.why: Shenandoah: alternative to ZGC for ultra-low latency. Epsilon: measure GC overhead, test allocation-free code, or short-lived batch jobs where GC never needed.
terms.how: -XX:+UseShenandoahGC — enable Shenandoah. -XX:+UseEpsilonGC — enable Epsilon (will OOM when heap full).
terms.where: Shenandoah: latency-critical microservices. Epsilon: performance benchmarks, allocation-analysis tools, test environments.
terms.when: Shenandoah: same use cases as ZGC — sub-1ms pauses. Epsilon: ONLY when you know heap will not fill during app lifetime.
terms.issue: Epsilon GC: heap fills and JVM crashes with OOM — no collection ever happens.
terms.fix: Only use Epsilon when you have measured that the app does not exceed heap capacity. All other cases: use G1GC or ZGC.

star.s: Interview: name two low-pause GC options in modern Java.
star.a: ZGC and Shenandoah — both aim for sub-millisecond pauses. Both concurrent. ZGC uses colored pointers, Shenandoah uses Brooks forwarding pointer.

subsections.title: Reference Types & Memory
subsections.keyRule: Reference strength: Strong > Soft > Weak > Phantom. Strong: never collected. Soft: collected when low memory. Weak: collected at next GC. Phantom: after finalization. finalize() is deprecated — avoid. Use Cleaner (Java 9+) for resource cleanup.

terms.what: A WeakReference holds a reference that GC can collect at any time during the next GC cycle, regardless of memory pressure.
terms.why: For caches where entries should disappear when no longer strongly referenced elsewhere — automatic eviction without explicit code.
terms.how: WeakReference<BigObject> ref = new WeakReference<>(object); BigObject obj = ref.get(); if(obj == null) { /* was collected */ }
terms.where: WeakHashMap (keys are weak — entries auto-removed when key not referenced). Caches. Canonicalization maps. String.intern() internals.
terms.when: Use WeakReference for caches where the cached object should be collected if no one is using it. Always check get() for null.
terms.issue: WeakReference cleared very aggressively — data may disappear too soon under load. Cache hit rate can drop.
terms.fix: For caches, SoftReference is usually better — collected only under memory pressure, not at every GC.

star.a: WeakReference cleared every GC cycle even with plenty of memory. Changed to SoftReference.
star.r: Cache retains entries until memory pressure. Hit rate improved.

terms.what: SoftReference is collected only when JVM is under memory pressure (low heap). JVM guarantees to collect all SoftReferences before throwing OutOfMemoryError.
terms.why: Perfect for memory-sensitive caches — entries stay as long as memory is available, evicted when memory gets tight.
terms.how: SoftReference<Image> ref = new SoftReference<>(image); Image img = ref.get(); if(img == null) { img = loadFromDisk(); ref = new SoftReference<>(img); }
terms.where: Image caches, document caches, compiled regex caches, any memory-sensitive cache.
terms.when: Use SoftReference for caches you want to stay as long as possible without risking OOM.
terms.issue: SoftReferences cleared aggressively when -Xmx is small. With a small heap, they get very little benefit.
terms.fix: Set -Xmx appropriately. Consider Caffeine cache with maximumSize + expireAfterAccess for production caches — better performance than manual SoftReference.

star.s: Image editor OOM loading large images.
star.a: SoftReference cache. Images stay in memory until JVM needs space.
star.r: No OOM. Images evicted gracefully when needed.

terms.what: PhantomReference.get() always returns null — you cannot access the object. Enqueued in a ReferenceQueue after object is finalized. Used for cleanup after finalization.
terms.why: Safe alternative to finalize() — get notification AFTER object is gone, then clean up native resources.
terms.how: ReferenceQueue<MyObj> queue = new ReferenceQueue<>(); PhantomReference<MyObj> ref = new PhantomReference<>(obj, queue); // When obj is GCd: queue.remove() unblocks.
terms.where: Native memory cleanup, file descriptor cleanup, any resource that needs post-GC cleanup.
terms.when: Use PhantomReference (or Java 9's Cleaner) for native resource cleanup. Never use finalize().
terms.issue: PhantomReference processing requires a background thread polling the ReferenceQueue — extra complexity.
terms.fix: Java 9+ Cleaner is easier: Cleaner.create(); cleaner.register(obj, cleanupAction); — Cleaner handles the background thread.

star.s: Interview: how to safely clean up off-heap memory when Java object is GCd?
star.a: PhantomReference + ReferenceQueue OR Java 9 Cleaner. finalize() is deprecated. Cleaner is the modern approach.

terms.what: finalize() is a method called by GC before collecting an object — intended for cleanup. It is deprecated since Java 9 and should never be used in new code.
terms.why: finalize() has serious problems: non-deterministic timing, can resurrect objects, slows GC, cannot guarantee execution. Caused countless bugs.
terms.how: @Override protected void finalize() { // NEVER DO THIS. resource.close(); } — bad. Use try-with-resources or Cleaner instead.
terms.where: Should never appear in new code. May appear in old legacy code — a warning sign.
terms.when: Never. Use try-with-resources for cleanup, AutoCloseable for resource classes, or Cleaner for post-GC native cleanup.
terms.issue: finalize() may not be called at all. Object may be resurrected in finalize(). GC pauses increase because GC must track finalizable objects.
terms.fix: Implement AutoCloseable. Use try-with-resources. For post-GC native cleanup, use Java 9 Cleaner.

star.s: Legacy class uses finalize() to close file handles. File handles leak.
star.a: Implemented AutoCloseable. Changed callers to try-with-resources. Removed finalize().

terms.what: A heap dump is a snapshot of all objects in the JVM heap at a moment in time. Used to diagnose memory leaks, OOM, and excessive object creation.
terms.why: When you have an OOM or growing memory, you need to see exactly what objects are in memory and what is holding them alive.
terms.how: Get heap dump: 1) Auto: -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError. 2) Manual: jmap - dump:format=b,file=heap.hprof <pid>. Analyze with: Eclipse Memory Analyzer (MAT), VisualVM, or JProfiler.
terms.where: Production post-mortem, performance investigation, memory leak diagnosis.
terms.when: Analyze heap dump when: memory grows without bound, OOM happens, specific object count grows unexpectedly.
terms.issue: Heap dump from a 32GB heap = 32GB file. Hard to transfer and analyze.
terms.fix: Use MAT's 'Leak Suspects Report' — it automatically identifies the biggest memory consumers and suspect paths. Focus on the dominator tree.

star.a: - XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError. Dump collected. MAT showed HttpSession holding 2M cached objects from background job never expiring. Fixed TTL.
